title: Continued Rapid Ohia Death Response Act of 2025
short_title: Continued Rapid Ohia Death Response Act of 2025
stage_label: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.
plain_summary: This bill requires government agencies to work together to fight a deadly tree disease called Rapid Ohia Death that is killing native Hawaiian trees. The disease is caused by a harmful fungus that spreads and destroys ohia trees, which are important to Hawaii's forests. Government scientists must continue studying how the disease spreads and control wild animals like deer that help spread it. The Forest Service must give money to help prevent the disease from spreading and to replant Hawaii's native forests.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Continued Rapid Ohia Death Response Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Definitions
sections.text: In this law, Rapid Ohia Death means a tree disease caused by a fungus called Ceratocystis fimbriata. This fungus kills ohia trees, which are a native Hawaiian tree species called Metrosideros polymorpha. When this law says State, it means the State of Hawaii.

sections.title: Section 3  Collaboration
sections.text: The Secretary of the Interior must work together with the Secretary of Agriculture and the State of Hawaii to fight Rapid Ohia Death.

sections.title: Section 4  Sustained Efforts
sections.text: The Secretary of the Interior, working through the United States Geological Survey, and the Forest Service Chief, working through the Forest Service Institute of Pacific Islands Forestry, must keep studying how Rapid Ohia Death spreads from place to place and how it moves between trees. The Secretary of the Interior, working through the United States Fish and Wildlife Service, must keep working with the Secretary of Agriculture, Hawaii, and local partners to control wild animals like pigs and goats in areas where they are fighting Rapid Ohia Death. This includes federal land, state land, and private land, but only if private landowners agree. The Secretary of Agriculture, working through the Forest Service Chief, must keep providing money to prevent Rapid Ohia Death from spreading and to help restore Hawaii's native forests. This includes money given through agreements with the Secretary of the Interior. The Secretary must also keep providing staff and money for buildings and equipment to the Institute of Pacific Islands Forestry so they can study Rapid Ohia Death.
